32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown

42

44	<slug>
45

47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49

52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>

56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```

63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
87	
88	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
89	
90	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
91	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
94	
95	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
96	
97	## What you must / must not do
98	
99	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
100	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
101	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
102	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
103	
104	## Workflow
105	
106	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
107	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
108	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
109	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
110	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
111	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
112	7. Write your report.

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.

72	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}

88	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
89	\begin{definition}\leanok
90	[Jacobian]
91	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}

93	  
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
95	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
96	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
97	\end{definition}

182	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
183	\begin{itemize}
184	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
185	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
186	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
187	\end{itemize}
188	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
189	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
190	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
191	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.

249	\begin{proof}
250	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
251	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
252	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
253	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
254	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
257	    \[
258	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
259	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
260	    \]
261	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
262	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
263	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
264	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
265	    \[
266	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
267	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
268	    \]
269	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).

29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.

35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.

471	[Genus-\(0\) Albanese witness]
472	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
473	  
474	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
475	  Let \(C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\). Then there exists a \(\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C\) whose underlying scheme is \(\Spec k\) (the terminal object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\)), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension \(0\), properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\) field encodes the Albanese universal property for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\).
476	\end{theorem}
477	\begin{proof}
478	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
479	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take \(J := \mathbf{1}\), the terminal object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\), which on underlying schemes is \(\Spec k\) viewed as a \(k\)-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
480	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object \(\mathbf{1}\) of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to \(\mathbf{1}\), and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism \(\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}\) is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:134}--\texttt{140}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on \(\Spec k\) from the fact that, on the geometric fibre \(\Spec K\) over any field extension \(k \to K\), the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and \(\Spec K\) is irreducible.
481	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension \(\genus\,C\).} The smoothness witness \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is required at relative dimension \(\genus\,C\). The hypothesis \(\genus\,C = 0\) rewrites the target relative dimension to \(0\), after which the goal reduces to \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})\). This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension \(0\).
482	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\), we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple \((C, P, J)\).
483	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map \(\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}\) (the structure morphism of \(C\) as a \(k\)-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition \(P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\) holds automatically: both sides are morphisms \(\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\), and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
484	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with identity element \(\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\) and a morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), we must exhibit a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = \alpha \circ g\). \emph{Existence}: take \(g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\). Then \(\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A\), which equals \(f\) precisely when \(f\) is the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\). We obtain this equality by descent from the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\). Base-change the data along \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\): the curve \(C_{\bar k}\) is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible of genus \(0\) (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve), the group scheme \(A_{\bar k}\) is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible, the base-changed point \(P_{\bar k}\) is a \(\bar k\)-rational point, and \(f_{\bar k}\) still kills it: \(f_{\bar k}(P_{\bar k}) = \eta_{A_{\bar k}}\). By \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (the project's characteristic-free genus-\(0\) rigidity keystone of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \emph{no} \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\) hypothesis; it lives in the upstream \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean}, imported by \texttt{Jacobian.lean}, so consuming it here introduces no import cycle), \(f_{\bar k}\) equals the constant morphism \(\mathtt{toUnit}\,C_{\bar k} \circ \eta_{A_{\bar k}}\) over \(\bar k\). Finally we descend this morphism equality back to \(k\): the projection \(p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C\) is the base change of the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), hence faithfully flat and surjective, hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; right-cancelling \(p\) against the base-change-square identities yields \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A\) as \(k\)-scheme morphisms (Sub-step~C.2.f of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}). \emph{Uniqueness}: if \(g_1, g_2 \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\) both satisfy \(f = \alpha \circ g_i\) with \(\alpha = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C\), then \(\alpha \circ g_1 = \alpha \circ g_2\), and we right-cancel \(\alpha\). This is the \emph{same} epi-cancellation as in the Existence paragraph: the underlying scheme morphism of \(\alpha\) is \(C.\mathtt{hom} \colon C \to \Spec k\) (\texttt{Over.toUnit\_left}), which is faithfully flat and surjective (a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve is faithfully flat and surjective over its base field), hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; therefore \(\alpha\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\) by \texttt{Over.epi\_of\_epi\_left}, and \texttt{cancel\_epi} gives \(g_1 = g_2\). Uniqueness does \emph{not} follow from terminality of \(\mathbf{1}\): morphisms \emph{out} of a terminal object are global points and are not unique in general (terminality gives uniqueness only of morphisms \emph{into} \(\mathbf{1}\)).
485	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the \(C(k) = \emptyset\) branch.} When no \(k\)-rational point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\) exists (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\) are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier \(\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\) is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's \(\forall\) over an empty type is trivially true. (The chart-algebra envelope of the fallback supplies only the converse implication \(df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constant, and carries a characteristic-\(p\) gap; the committed route~(c) avoids that gap entirely.)
487	\end{proof}

  edge would point `def:genus` at nodes that already depend on it

connected to the route head of the sub-DAG it actually lives in (the Cohomology
H¹ construction), so the goal cone reaches that sub-DAG's head. The RR↔genus

to add (boundary handoff below).
